claim: A humane trap for a lost dog should only be deployed after the dog has been confirmed eating at a specific food station on a trail camera — not before.
stance_target: trap deployment as a Bayesian posterior action triggered by confirmed sighting/eating evidence
scope_conditions: All lost dogs requiring trapping; exception for gregarious dogs who can be lured without traps
quoted_support: When do you set a trap for a lost dog? When you have already done the following: You have set up a food station with a trail camera. Then you have confirmed your dog was there eating the food.

claim: Not all lost dogs need trapping — gregarious dogs can be lured via calming signals; trapping is reserved for dogs that cannot be approached.
stance_target: temperament-stratified recovery protocol — lure vs. trap decision
scope_conditions: Gregarious dogs; experienced MAR-trained individual required for luring
quoted_support: Not every dog needs to be trapped. What we mean is that trapping a dog using a humane trap is safe but can be startling to a skittish dog. If you have a very friendly dog most of the time, they can be lured using calming signals instead of being trapped.

claim: Humane traps must be monitored every 1-3 hours (or via cellular game camera); no animal should remain in a trap more than 30 minutes.
stance_target: animal welfare constraint on trap deployment protocol
quoted_support: Traps should be checked every 1-3 hours if you are not placing a wireless camera to watch the trap remotely... Ideally, you don't want any animal in a trap for over 30 minutes if possible.

claim: Overnight trap management: tie trap door open with bungee cord so dog can enter, eat, and habituate to the trap without being captured — increasing comfort for next triggered deployment.
stance_target: desensitization protocol for trap-shy dogs during unmonitored overnight periods
scope_conditions: When trap cannot be monitored overnight
quoted_support: If you cannot stay and monitor the trap overnight... we recommend refreshing the smelly food in the trap and then tying the door with a bungee cord or rope. This will allow the lost pet to get some food and to see the trap is less threatening for when it is set to go off again.
